one_line_takeaway: Anthropic's successor Constitutional Classifier system combines context-aware exchange classifiers, a cost-gated two-stage cascade, and novel SWiM-smoothed softmax-weighted linear probes to cut jailbreak-defense compute overhead by ~40x and production refusal rate to 0.05% (from 0.38%) while achieving the lowest measured vulnerability-discovery rate (0.00505/1000 queries) across four internally compared systems.
key_finding: The final production-grade system reduces compute overhead by ~40x relative to the single exchange classifier (Section 3) and 8x relative to the non-probe two-stage cascade (Section 4), while achieving a 0.05% production flag/refusal rate (vs 0.38% reported for the original Constitutional Classifiers) and the lowest vulnerability discovery rate of any system tested: 0.00505 high-risk vulnerabilities per thousand red-teaming queries (only 1 high-risk vulnerability found in 198K queries over 1,736 red-team hours), versus 0.01871 for the last-generation system (13 vulnerabilities/695K queries), 0.00885 for the exchange-only classifier (2/226K), and 0.00878 for the non-probe two-stage cascade (5/569K). No red-teamer found a universal jailbreak against the final system able to answer all 8 CBRN target questions at half the detail of an unsafeguarded model. Separately, the exchange-classifier architecture alone yielded 2.2x fewer high-risk vulnerabilities per attempt than the dual input/output-classifier baseline. In the probe ablations, combining SWiM smoothing with softmax-weighted loss gave the lowest ASR (~1.4% using all layers), beating either technique alone or neither; using fewer probe layers monotonically worsened ASR. Probe-plus-small-external-classifier ensembles outperformed even the two-external-classifier ensemble and exceeded the small classifier's robustness alone while cutting compute by 100x in the isolated probe-cascade analysis (Fig. 3c), attributed to lower prediction correlation (rank correlation) between the probe and external classifier than between pairs of external classifiers.
core_contribution: Anthropic presents 'Constitutional Classifiers++', a successor to the original Constitutional Classifiers (Sharma et al., 2025) jailbreak-defense system for LLMs, contributing three own techniques combined into a production system: (1) 'exchange classifiers' that evaluate model outputs jointly with their full input context (replacing separate input-only and output-only classifiers) to resist reconstruction and output-obfuscation attacks; (2) a two-stage classifier cascade in which a cheap first-stage classifier screens all traffic and escalates only flagged exchanges to an expensive second-stage classifier, cutting compute cost while tolerating a higher first-stage false-positive rate; and (3) lightweight linear activation probes trained with two novel loss modifications the authors introduce -- Sliding Window Mean (SWiM) logit smoothing and a softmax-weighted BCE loss -- which are ensembled with a small fine-tuned external classifier. These are combined into a final production system (a weighted two-stage cascade: linear probe first stage, probe+external-classifier ensemble second stage, zfinal = 0.55*zprobe + 0.45*zclassifier) deployed to guard Claude Sonnet 4.5.
threat_model: Adversaries attempting 'universal jailbreaks' -- prompting strategies that consistently bypass LLM safeguards to extract detailed harmful (specifically CBRN-related: chemical, biological, radiological, nuclear) information across a majority of target queries in a domain -- against a deployed LLM protected by an output/exchange classification safeguard. The paper's own red-teaming first identifies two novel attack classes against the prior-generation system: (a) reconstruction attacks, which fragment a harmful query across benign-looking segments (e.g., functions scattered through a fake codebase) that the model reassembles and answers, evading input classifiers; and (b) output obfuscation attacks, which have the model answer using metaphor/coded substitution (e.g., disguising a request to synthesize hydrochloric acid as a 'riddle' about 'winter's breath') so the output looks benign to an output-only classifier that lacks input context. Defenders are assumed to control the classifier architecture and training data but must operate under strict production constraints: minimizing both compute overhead and refusal (false-positive) rate on benign traffic (calibrated against WildChat).
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) footnote 3 states expert-level red-teamers operating outside the bounty program (unlike the compensated internal red-teaming pool) nonetheless found universal jailbreaks against the two-stage cascade system, though 'requiring substantial effort and automated tools' -- a caveat the paper does not extend explicitly to the final production system. (2) Red-teaming effort (query volume and hours) differed substantially across the four systems compared (226K-695K queries), so the authors rely on a normalized 'discovery rate' metric, which they acknowledge is imperfect because other metrics (mean/min discovery time, raw vulnerability counts) have a 'much stronger dependence on the volume of red-teaming.' (3) The probe evaluation notes higher flag rates on benign scientific requests for the probe versus external classifiers. (4) Footnote 6 states the evaluation may underestimate attack success rate because an attack is scored as defended if the classifier flags at any point during generation, even after harmful content has already streamed. (5) The exchange-classifier-only comparison in Section 3 is confounded because it protects a different underlying model (Opus 4) than the last-gen baseline (Sonnet 3.7), a limitation the authors acknowledge but argue is minor. (6) The Future Work section explicitly flags that classifier-model integration, obfuscation-resistant model training, and further false-positive-rate reduction via targeted synthetic data remain open.

datasets_used: WildChat (Zhao et al., 2024) - used for calibrating classifier refusal-rate thresholds (0.1% flag rate)
GPQA Diamond (Rein et al., 2023) - used to measure capability degradation from obfuscation attacks on Claude Opus 4
Internal synthetic CBRN-constitution training data (following Sharma et al., 2025 pipeline) for classifier fine-tuning
~7,000-exchange static jailbreak dataset drawn from human red-teaming (held out from training) for probe/classifier ASR evaluation, truncated to 20K-token context
Live Claude Sonnet 4.5 production traffic (shadow deployment, Dec 1 2025-Jan 1 2026) for flag-rate measurement

Human red-teaming campaigns (bounty-based, via HackerOne, UK AISI, US CAISI, FAR.AI) across four system variants: 695K queries (last-gen), 226K (exchange classifier), 569K (two-stage cascade), 198K (production-grade)
quantitative_results: Production-grade system: 0.05% production flag/refusal rate vs 0.38% for original Constitutional Classifiers (Sharma et al. 2025)
Production-grade system: 40x compute reduction vs single exchange classifier (Section 3); 8x reduction vs non-probe two-stage cascade (Section 4)

High-risk vulnerability discovery rate per 1000 queries: Last-Gen 0.01871 (13 vulns/695K); Exchange Classifier 0.00885 (2/226K); Two-Stage Cascade 0.00878 (5/569K); Production-Grade 0.00505 (1/198K, Table 1 lists as 0.00505; text says 'corresponding to a detection rate of 0.005')
Relative compute overhead (Table 1, vs Last-Gen=100%): Exchange Classifier 150%; Two-Stage Cascade 27.8%; Production-Grade 3.5%
Production traffic refusal rate (Table 1): Last-Gen 0.073%; Exchange Classifier 0.038%; Two-Stage Cascade 0.036%; Production-Grade 0.050%
Exchange classifier vs last-gen dual classifier: 2.2x fewer high-risk vulnerabilities per jailbreaking attempt
Capability degradation under obfuscation (Claude Opus 4 on GPQA Diamond): 74.2% baseline accuracy -> 32.3% under first attack, 49% under second attack
Second-stage classifier update alone (Haiku 4.5 + pipeline optimizations) yielded a 3x compute reduction within the two-stage system
Two-stage cascade: first-stage screens 100% of traffic, ~10% escalated to second stage, first stage accounts for ~77% of total system cost

Best linear probe (all layers, SWiM + softmax loss): ~1.4% attack success rate on static jailbreak eval, competitive with the small (Haiku 4.5) external classifier and better than the extra-small classifier
Probe + small-external-classifier ensemble: exceeds robustness of small classifier alone while giving a 100x compute reduction (isolated probe-cascade analysis, Fig. 3c)
Production-grade final ensemble weighting: zfinal = 0.55*zprobe + 0.45*zclassifier; probe escalates ~5.5% of traffic to second stage
Final red-teaming: 1,736 cumulative hours across ~198K queries, one high-risk vulnerability found (discovery time 30 hours, the highest minimum discovery time of any system)

Last-gen system: 4 universal jailbreaks found, average discovery time 27 hours; exchange-classifier system: 1 universal jailbreak, 100-hour discovery time; two-stage and production-grade systems: no universal jailbreak achieving the full-detail threshold on all 8 target questions was found by bounty red-teamers
Linear probe compute cost estimate: all-layer probe on Gemma 3 27B (L=46, d=4096) requires ~377K FLOPs/token vs ~8B FLOPs/token for a Gemma 3 4B forward pass
baselines_compared: Last-generation Constitutional Classifiers (Sharma et al., 2025): separate input classifier (fine-tuned Claude Haiku 3.5) + streaming output-only classifier (smaller internal LLM), guarding Claude Sonnet 3.7
Exchange-only classifier (Section 3): single Haiku-3.5-based classifier evaluating output-in-context-of-input, guarding Claude Opus 4
Two-stage exchange classifier cascade without probes (Section 4): internal lightweight first-stage model (~1/3 size) + Haiku-4.5-based second-stage classifier
Extra-small (XS) external classifier and Small (S, Haiku 4.5) external classifier, compared individually and in an XS-S ensemble against the linear probe and probe-based ensembles
Loss-function ablations: smoothing-only, softmax-only, neither, both (probe training)
Layer-selection ablations: all layers vs every-second vs every-fourth layer (probe architecture)
OpenAI's adaptive topic-filter systems (ChatGPT Agent / GPT-5 system cards) - discussed as related work, not benchmarked head-to-head

Cunningham et al. (2025) internals-based last-N-layer classifiers and McKenzie et al. (2025) softmax-weighted probes - discussed as related/contemporaneous work, methodologically distinguished (streaming vs single sequence-level prediction) but not directly re-run as numeric baselines in this paper's tables
